turns.student: What brings you in today?
turns.patient: I've been experiencing a persistent productive cough, started dry and became productive with yellowish-green sputum over the past 5 days. It's really worrying me because I also have difficulty breathing and wheezing, and a low-grade fever that's intermittent but worse in the evenings.

turns.student: Can you tell me more about what you've been feeling?
turns.patient: It's just so scary to think about what might be going on inside my body. I feel like my chest is tight all the time, and when I try to take deep breaths it feels like there's a knife stabbing at me - that's how sharp the pain is, especially with coughing or laughing.

turns.student: When you have those episodes, do you notice anything else happening to your body?
turns.patient: I've been experiencing severe fatigue and malaise, to the point where I had to miss three days of work. And honestly, my appetite has just plummeted over the past five days - I used to love cooking, but now even the thought of eating feels overwhelming.

turns.student: Any sweating or clamminess when the symptoms hit?
turns.patient: Yes, I've been having night sweats every single night for four nights now, and it's like my pajamas are soaked through by morning. It's really unsettling to be experiencing this kind of intensity, especially since I'm usually not someone who gets hot or sweaty easily.

turns.student: So no perspiration at all during the episodes?
turns.patient: No, actually, it's quite the opposite - I've been feeling clammy and sweating profusely, especially in the evenings. It's like my body is just trying to regulate itself, but it feels really out of control.

turns.student: Does the discomfort stay in one spot, or does it move around?
turns.patient: The pain is mostly centered on my right side, and it gets worse when I breathe deeply or cough. It's a sharp stabbing sensation that makes me feel like I'm going to pass out sometimes.

turns.student: Does it radiate anywhere — like your arms, neck, or jaw?
turns.patient: The pain is mostly confined to my chest and right side, but occasionally it will radiate up into my shoulders and down into my back. Sometimes I feel a tightness in my throat too, especially when I try to swallow.

turns.student: Any tingling or numbness in your arms when it happens?
turns.patient: No, I don't think so - at least not that I've noticed. It's mostly just the sharp pain and tightness in my chest and right side that I'm aware of during those episodes.

turns.student: What about your extremities — any tingling, pain, or unusual sensations?
turns.patient: I don't experience any numbness or tingling in my arms, but sometimes when I have these episodes, my hands feel a bit cold and clammy. It's hard to describe, but it's like my circulation just gets really poor during those moments.

turns.student: Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Ankles, feet, hands?
turns.patient: No, I don't think so - I haven't experienced any noticeable swelling in my ankles, feet, or hands. But to be honest, it's hard for me to focus on anything other than these symptoms and how they're affecting my daily life right now. It's just really scary to feel this unwell and not know what's causing it.

turns.student: Do your shoes feel tighter than usual, or any puffiness in your legs?
turns.patient: No, my shoes don't feel tighter than usual, but I have been feeling a bit more short of breath when I'm walking, like my body is working harder to get oxygen. It's really frustrating because I used to be so active and now even just doing everyday tasks feels exhausting.

turns.student: Any stomach issues during the episodes? Nausea, queasiness, upset stomach?
turns.patient: To be honest, I've had some nausea and queasiness when these symptoms are at their worst, especially after coughing or vomiting up that yellowish-green sputum. It's like my body is just feeling overwhelmed and everything inside me is rebelling against it.

turns.student: Do you feel sick to your stomach when the symptoms come on?
turns.patient: Yes, I've been experiencing nausea and queasiness, especially after coughing or vomiting up that yellowish-green sputum. It's like my body is just feeling overwhelmed and everything inside me is rebelling against it.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers, chills, or night sweats?
turns.patient: I've been experiencing a low-grade fever, around 38.2°C (100.8°F), that's intermittent but worse in the evenings. And yes, I've also been having severe night sweats every single night for four nights now, like my pajamas are soaked through by morning.

turns.student: Have you noticed any changes in your appetite or weight?
turns.patient: My appetite has actually decreased significantly over the past 5 days, and I've been feeling really fatigued and malaise. To be honest, even thinking about food feels overwhelming right now, which is not like me at all - I usually love cooking and trying new recipes.

turns.student: Tell me about your lifestyle — diet, exercise, daily routine.
turns.patient:
I've been really struggling to keep up with my usual routine. As an elementary school teacher, I'm around young children every day, which can be a bit challenging when I'm feeling unwell like this. My diet has actually been pretty good lately - I've been trying to stick to healthy snacks and meals, but it's hard to have much of an appetite with the way I've been feeling. As for exercise, I usually try to get in a few yoga sessions or walks during my lunch break, but these past few days have been tough because just getting out of bed feels like an accomplishment.

I do take cetirizine as needed for my seasonal allergies, and I've also been taking over-the-counter dextromethorphan for the cough, but it hasn't been providing much relief. Socially, I'm not really feeling up to much - even seeing friends or family members is a bit overwhelming right now because I just want to be left alone with my thoughts. It's hard to explain, but this whole experience has left me feeling really anxious and withdrawn.

Lately, I've been trying to listen to calming music or nature sounds before bed in an attempt to regulate my breathing and heart rate, but it's not always working. Overall, my lifestyle is pretty routine-based - wake up early, teach a few classes, come home, cook dinner... but this whole episode has really disrupted that for me, and I'm just trying to focus on getting better one day at a time.

turns.student: How active are you physically? Do you work out or walk regularly?
turns.patient: I used to be pretty active - I'd try to get in a few yoga sessions or walks during my lunch break, but lately, just getting out of bed has been an accomplishment. The thought of exercising feels overwhelming when I'm feeling this unwell, and even simple tasks like walking feel exhausting.

turns.student: What does a typical day of eating look like for you?
turns.patient: To be honest, my appetite has really taken a hit over the past 5 days. Even thinking about food feels overwhelming to me right now. I usually try to stick to healthy snacks and meals, but lately, I've just been grabbing whatever is easiest or quickest, which isn't exactly ideal for someone who loves cooking like I do. Today, for instance, I ended up having a bowl of cereal for breakfast because it was just too much effort to make something more substantial.

turns.student: Any medical problems in your family? Parents, siblings?
turns.patient: My mother has asthma, and to be honest, that's one of the things that's been worrying me - I've always thought about how my own respiratory health might be connected to hers. It's funny, because when I was a kid, she'd always say "take deep breaths" when I got anxious or scared, but now it feels like her condition is coming back to haunt me in a way...

turns.student: Specifically, has anyone in your family had serious diseases?
turns.patient: My maternal grandmother passed away from lung cancer. It's been weighing on my mind lately, especially with these symptoms and how they're affecting me. The thought of potentially having something related to her condition is terrifying.
